comments.author: @JiaurRahman-h5i
comments.text:
Subject: Humble Request for Financial Assistance for Marriage

Assalamu Alaikum Wa Rahmatullahi Wa Barakatuh,

I hope this message finds you in good health and strong faith.

My name is Jiaur Rahman, and I am from Tangkhali, Ukhia, Teknaf, Cox's Bazar, Bangladesh. I am currently a student studying in a madrasa and striving to continue my Islamic education.

I am writing to humbly request your financial assistance regarding my marriage. Due to my family's difficult financial condition, I am unable to arrange the necessary expenses for marriage on my own. As a madrasa student, I have very limited income, and what little support I receive is not sufficient to meet these expenses.

In our area, the marriage-related costs, including mahr and other essential arrangements, are comparatively high. Because of these circumstances, I have been unable to proceed with marriage despite my sincere desire to do so in a lawful and honorable manner.

I have sought assistance from various sources, but unfortunately I have not received the support needed. Therefore, I am turning to kind-hearted brothers and sisters in the hope that someone may be willing to help me during this difficult time.

Any amount of assistance, no matter how small, would be greatly appreciated and would help me take an important step toward establishing a halal family life.

May Allah (SWT) reward you abundantly for your generosity, kindness, and support. I sincerely ask for your prayers as well.

Jazakum Allahu Khairan.

Sincerely,

Jiaur RahmanTangkhali, Ukhia, Teknaf, Cox's Bazar, BangladeshWhatsApp: +8801854699710

comments.author: @Howwie79
comments.text: Try living here when its raining, sleeting, hailing and the wind is stormy. Try that for 3 months in a row and tell me how beautiful it is. I love Norway and have lived here for 47 years. Spring and summer lasts like 3 months if you are very fortunate. The rest of the year is like I said in the start, of course depends on where in Norway you live. This video only shows the good parts, and when we have summer and spring It Is amazingly beautiful indeed. Welcome to any and all who wants to visit. I'd say best time would me mid June until September. Greetings from a middle-aged grumpy Norwegian man 😂❤

comments.author: @johnnymartinjohansen
comments.text:
"In summer, the sun sometimes shines for 24 hours"

Ehhhh... Up here in the north, if the weather is good, the sun can be shining for weeks without break. Where I live in Nordre Nordland, the sun stays above the horizon for 7-8 weeks.

"More than half of all newly registered cars are electric". Wrong. It's almost 100%, since new cars must be emission free.

Pro tip for anyone wanting to go to Lofoten:

Do NOT come in the high season (late May to mid/late August). It's extremely overcrowded and everything is expensive as hell.

comments.author: @runedahl1477
comments.text: You missed what lies between Trondheim and Lofoten. Near Brønnøysund lies a mountain Torghatten that has a large hole right through it. In folklore this mountain is said to be the hat of a troll that an other troll shot an arrow through. Then you have Svartisen, Norway second largest glacier, that goes all the way down to the fjord. Two hours drive away from that you have Saltstraumen that is the strongest tidal current in the world. On the island of Lovund the puffins returns in the thousands every year at the same date. Near Narvik you will find Stetind, Norway’s national mountain. The shape of this mountain is spectacular from the fjord and to the top where it’s gets steeper and steeper. Every autumn the fjord is visited by orcas in large numbers. It is also here where Norway is at its most narrow, only 6 kilometers to the Swedish border. The region has the densest concentration of sea eagles in Europe and probably in the world. In Finnmark there is a place where the borders of Norway,Finland and Russia meets. It is probably the only spot in the world that has three different time zones depending on which side of the border you stand. It is recommended not to cross the border even if it is just one meter.😊😊😊

comments.author: @youtor9900
comments.text:
Norway is magic! I moved to the US in 1983-84 as a 23 yo in 2025 my husband and I did a 11 week trip to places I had not been to. I had just retired. So I wanted to get to know the country, it was my #1 bucket list, feeling I knew so much about it, but hadn’t seen it. At first we tried a totally green approach, but in The northernmost area-uplifting transportation is limited. Happy to visit family and friends, so happy! Showing my mother’s birthplace, all the sight in the Porsanger area. The North Cape was a total delight! In Gjesvær, there is a birdwatching trip to behold and remember for a lifetime! As far as traveling here, we decided for a rental car starting and ending in Hammerfest.This video does not show the eastern part of Finnmark, where I grew up, but I recommend a trip to Kirkenes, for it’s unique historical and geographical location, and to Vardø for it’s Hornøya 
Bird mountain.

comments.text:
Oslo is a pos city. I worked there for 6 months, never ever again. I don't live far from Oslo, but hell no will I travel back to that sh1tdump of a city  again. If I must, it's because I'm forced to do so. I haven't experienced a more cold and unfriendly people in my life.
Living standards in norway is great, if you are billionaire. If not, good luck affording a decent life. When it comes to this EV car hoax, just wait and see, itt will go pretty much like the diesel hoax we went through. When enough people depend on these cars, the charging cost will sky rocket and the fee's of these cars will reduce it to only the richest among us can afford cars. We will depend on this wreck of our train and bus system we've got. 11 months and 3 weeks of the year it barely function. The last week, if we are extremly lucky, it's bus for train half the time.

replies.author: @Fiskeflett1
@knutarvidhansen3300
replies.text: @Fiskeflett1 Jeg kan bekrefte at du har noe rett. Når oslofolk kommer utenbygds (på landet) så tror de at de har rett til å ta seg til rette i hva som helst! Da er vi lokale undermåls, mens de tar seg til rette uten å tenke på å innrette seg. Jeg bor 45 km nordvest oslo rett ved et populært utsiktspunkt (og flysted for glidere).

replies.author: @Fiskeflett1
replies.text: @Scento-b4c Ja, Oslo er en fantastisk flott by, men mange av innbyggerne kunne virkelig ha trengt min kjære bestefars oppdragelse i anstendig folkeskikk. Det er jeg ikke alene om å mene eller ha opplevd. Om jeg passer inn, har ingenting å si; min oppførsel endrer seg ikke fra det ene stedet til det andre. Jeg kan forstå at det å kritisere Oslo så sterkt som jeg gjorde, kan oppleves som utfordrende og som et «smikk på nesen». Men som sagt: Det jeg snakker om, er generell oppførsel. I løpet av seks måneder får man et generelt inntrykk av menneskene der.Jeg beveger meg i en retning eller står stille der jeg står. Der jeg kommer fra, er jeg vant til at hvis man dytter, dulter eller skubber borti noen, så sier man av naturlig høflighet «unnskyld» eller «beklager». Det hørte jeg sjelden fra dem som gjorde dette i Oslo. Der var det heller øretelefoner på og et skarpt blikk rett mot deg hvis du så i deres retning.Nei, jeg har ingenting imot annerledeshet. Vær så annerledes du bare vil, men vær så snill: En liten tanke anstendighet og gjenkjennbarhet av en tålelig god oppdragelse hadde vært hyggelig. Jeg snakker ikke om Oslo som geografisk sted eller utseendemessig, men om at byen – gjennom noen av dem som bor der – oppleves som kald og uvelkommen. Dette er ikke en opplevelse jeg er alene om å ha.Når det gjelder et potensielt «lureri» angående fremtidige elbiler, kan man se tilbake til 2007. Da ble folk anbefalt å kjøpe dieselbil, før myndighetene snudde og økte avgiftene kraftig. Folk som hadde fulgt anbefalingene, følte seg med rette lurt, da både annenhåndsverdi og bruksverdi ble kraftig redusert. Hva skjer i 2028? Vil ikke elbiler få full mva. fra 1. januar 2028?Elbillading hjemme blir øremerket «elbillading». Når fossilbilene er borte, hvilken bilgruppe må da dekke inn avgiftstapet den norske stat vil få? Jo, elbilene, for de er de eneste som står igjen. Det har skjedd før, så hvorfor skulle det ikke skje igjen – at de øker avgiftene fordi det påstås at det er et avgiftstap som må dekkes opp? For eksempel gjennom en type drivstoffavgift via ladestasjonen hjemme. Her i Norge blir bil fremdeles ansett som en luksusgjenstand fra politisk hold. Derav det som tidligere var kjent som bilavgift, men som nå kalles veibruksavgift. Tar jeg feil?

replies.author: @Scento-b4c
replies.text: @Fiskeflett1 Oslo er en by som kan by på ALT, sommer som vinter! Du passet ikke til Oslo, og det er helt i orden! For øvrig er din el-bil-teori bare tøv!

replies.author: @johnnymartinjohansen
replies.text: @Fiskeflett1 I'm from a very small place in Nordland. It's almost "tradition" up north to dislike Oslo, but my personal experience is that they're different in many ways, but just as polite and friendly. That's why I strongly believe you're the problem, not them.

replies.author: @Fiskeflett1
replies.text:
@johnnymartinjohansen 
How am I the problem? I’m just acting the same way I do back home. My experience is what it is.I didn't have any expectations before this six-month stay. I’m from a small town where we say 'Hi' or 'Good morning' with a smile, just to be friendly. That’s my 'default' setting for meeting people, and it's how I was raised. Sure, this place is nowhere near the size of Oslo, but a little politeness goes a long way. I’m not acting any differently in the city than I do at home.

replies.author: @Fiskeflett1
replies.text:
@panavia86 
I'm not talking about the Carbon fotprint. What I'm talking about, regarding the "hoax", is compareable diesel thing that happened a few years back. Where our goverment advised us to buy diesel cars, the next year they increased thetaxes and  fee's on these cars, also the fuel fee's. That's why today, we have those prices we have. That's the comparison I make regarding the future of EV-cars. Among other things, but I don't want make this text as long as an essay.
If Oslo city isn't as bad as it's experienced, why is it so then, that people HAVE this experience of Oslo? You say it your self, "I dont like Oslo much either, but it's not that bad."

This is the reason "Why", I quit my job in Oslo. Good paying job, nice place to work. But "No thanks".

replies.author: @Fiskeflett1
replies.text:
​@fredrikrustad2930 
Hvordan kan det være min feil? Min hverdagslige væremåte er rett og slett slik: Møter man noen, eller havner i en situasjon hvor et «hei, god dag» og et påfølgende hyggelig smil er naturlig, så velger jeg dette som en form for «default». Slik er hverdagen der jeg kommer fra. Det er riktignok et noe mindre sted enn Oslo, men likevel. Høflighet og dannelse er ikke noe jeg forventer, eller på noe vis føler meg berettiget til, men det er slik jeg er.
Vil du virkelig påstå at kollektivsystemet utenfor Oslo fungerer optimalt? Selv Jernbaneverket (eller Bane Nor) har gått ut og sagt at de vil holde tilbake informasjon om togtrøbbel fordi det skaper så mye «støy». Er det virkelig noe som får en til å si: «Jaggu, dette funker finfint!»?
Min opplevelse av mennesker i Oslo er nå bare slik den er. Jeg søker ikke etter å bli dullet med – jeg jobber med lager og logistikk, så det å få en kommentar slengt etter meg eller litt røff tone er en del av arbeidshverdagen – men litt høflighet og et lite smil hadde hjulpet mye. På vei ut av T-bane eller buss blir man dyttet og dultet borti, men hører man noen gang et «unnskyld»? Det skjer uhyre sjelden. Det er enten «vekk fra min vei» eller «dra til h******».
Det var enkelte hyggelige opplevelser også, jada. At den forrige teksten var skrevet med en smule overdrivelse? Ja, til en viss grad. Men den er ikke ukorrekt når det gjelder de hverdagslige opplevelsene. Opplevelsen min var glassklar, og jeg er ikke alene om å ha det slik. Jeg har både bekjente og familiemedlemmer som vegrer seg for å reise til Oslo fordi byen oppleves som kald og uvelkommen. Hvis noen av dem hadde skrevet denne teksten, kan jeg love deg at den hadde vært mange hakk hvassere.

comments.author: @Kamasutrawhat
comments.text: Great video, but just a small correction: starting in Oslo isn't actually starting at the southernmost point! That’s about a 5-hour drive further south. By skipping Sørlandet (Southern Norway), you’re missing the 'Nordic Riviera.' It’s where us Norwegians actually go on holiday! Towns like Mandal, Lillesand, and Kristiansand with their white wooden houses and archipelagos are criminally overlooked by international tourists. Definitely worth a detour next time!

replies.author: @user-gr5tx6rd4h
replies.text:
@ahkkariq7406 
In addition the word "fjord" is also used for some long, narrow lakes in Norway, like Sperillen and Randsfjorden etc.

replies.author: @ahkkariq7406
replies.text: If you take the definition of a fjord, "a deep, narrow and elongated ocean channel with steep mountain sides on three sides, formed by glaciers digging out U-shaped valleys during the ice ages", then no. The word fjord is Norwegian, and in the Norwegian language fjord also includes ocean channels without the characteristic, steep mountain sides - so it is debatable. Norwegians will absolutely say that the Oslofjord is a fjord, but in geological terms it is not strictly a fjord. Then the question is which came first, a term that includes both variants or the definition of the term?

replies.author: @ThinkHEAVEN-YouTube

replies.text: Yes, Oslo is situated at the bottom (north end) of a fjord, Oslofjorden. The third biggest city, Trondheim, known for its gorgeous, ancient cathedral, is also situated at a fjord (Trondheimsfjorden, a pretty long one), but not at the end of it.
